model_narrative: We are choosing how to allocate money across a set of available bonds. For each bond we decide how much to invest, and we also track the total amount invested and the portfolio's total after-tax return. Each bond has its own credit rating, maturity, gross yield, and tax rate. The objective is to maximize the total after-tax return of the portfolio.

sets.name: b
sets.doc: the available bonds that can be invested in

sets.name: g
sets.doc: a designated subset of the bonds forming a group that carries a minimum-investment requirement

params.name: rating
params.doc: the credit rating score of each bond, where a lower number is a safer bond

params.name: maturity
params.doc: the maturity of each bond in years

params.name: yld
params.doc: the gross yield of each bond stated as a percentage, so a value of 5 means five percent

params.name: taxrate
params.doc: the fraction of each bond's yield that is lost to tax, between zero and one

vars.name: investment
vars.doc: the amount invested in each bond

vars.name: tinvest
vars.doc: the total amount invested across all bonds, bounded between zero and ten

vars.name: ret
vars.doc: the total after-tax return of the portfolio

constraints.name: investment[g]
constraints.intent: The bonds in the designated group must together receive a sufficient allocation. The combined amount invested across the bonds in that group must be at least the required minimum level.

constraints.name: rating[b]
constraints.intent: The portfolio must not be too risky on average. Weighting each bond's investment by its credit rating and adding these up, the total must not exceed the total amount invested scaled by the highest average rating the portfolio is allowed to carry.

constraints.name: maturity[b]
constraints.intent: The portfolio must not be too long-dated on average. Weighting each bond's investment by its maturity and adding these up, the total must not exceed the total amount invested scaled by the longest average maturity the portfolio is allowed to carry.

constraints.name: tinvest
constraints.intent: The total amount invested must be consistent with the individual allocations. The total investment equals the sum of the amounts invested across all bonds.

constraints.name: ret
constraints.intent: The total after-tax return must be consistent with the individual allocations. For each bond, take the amount invested, apply the bond's yield expressed as a percentage, and keep only the portion that survives tax. The total after-tax return equals the sum of these amounts across all bonds.

query: There's a proposal on the table to spread out the government savings exposure — the combined stake in the two series bonds, us-ser-e and us-ser-f, shouldn't run past 6 between them. What does the optimizer return for the after-tax return and the resulting mix?

query: We're looking at a tax-exempt sleeve rule: the money parked in the two municipal names, municip-a and municip-b together, should stay within a quarter of the total book that actually gets deployed. Plug it in and walk me through the new return and allocation.

query: The board is pitching a two-part credit overlay for the next review: carry at least 1 in the corporate name so we keep a foothold in that issuer, and at the same time cap the combined series-bond holding, us-ser-e plus us-ser-f, at 6. What does the optimizer return for the after-tax return once both are in?

query: The book leans hard on municip-a and barely touches municip-b, even though municip-b is the richer tax-free name on an after-tax basis. Shouldn't we at least hold as much of municip-b as we do of municip-a? What's keeping the model from balancing the two municipals that way?

query: The allocation piles a huge slug into one or two names and that concentration makes me nervous. Couldn't we just put a uniform single-issuer ceiling in place — no individual bond over 3 — across every name on the sheet? What's the tradeoff the model is making by stacking up on a single position instead?

query: us-ser-e dominates the savings-bond side of the book relative to municip-a sitting alongside it. Wouldn't it be more even-handed to keep us-ser-e to no more than half of what those two names hold between them? What's the model seeing that pushes it so far into us-ser-e?

query: Almost the whole book ends up sitting in the grouped government-and-corporate names while the tax-free municipals get crowded out. Couldn't we just hold that grouped sleeve — corporate, us-ser-e and us-ser-f combined — to 5 at most? What's pulling the model so heavily toward that group?
expected_pyomo: model.group_sleeve_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.investment[g] for g in model.g) <= 5.0)
